turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Solicitor General, you may proceed.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: May it please the Court. I'll try to answer Mr. Justice Frankfurter's question first. I think there's no problem at all about the question that you asked. Section 503 of the 1940 Act which would be controlling under all of these cases, in 54 statutes at large at page 1171, provides for an independent trial de novo on the question of nationality

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: -- and it's been uniformly so construed. So that -- and in Nishikawa case in the transcript shows that that's the Section under which the action is brought in specific terms and the rest of them were brought under that provision of the statute so that this Section gives them a trial de novo in accordance with the requirements of due process.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: They let you say here, isn't it? And they do a redundance in saying and that's part of reality so that in answer to my question why shouldn't this have the procedure that was constitutionally required in being controlled you could see now, you say the answer is the judge.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: In pretrial.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Do you say it has to decide for them?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: I'd have to compare to know that --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: What rights does he have?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: He has a trial de novo on the question --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Before whom?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Before a District Court of the United States --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Is that all that --

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: -- for the District of Columbia or in the District Court of the United States for the district in which such person claims a permanent residence for a judgment declaring him to be a national of the United States.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: You think that would supply all the former cases if you have the -- protect -- the procedural protection to which that case has indicated that citizens should have?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: I guess in the trial de novo on --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: For judgment.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Yes, sir. And I would think that would satisfy all the constitutional requirements in regard to --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: -- (Voice Overlap) when a man is being deprived of something to get a case -- I suppose a case of controversy. Some people might think he ought to have a jury.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Well, he doesn't get it under our practice in many situations and it still complies with due process according --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Well, it depends on the definition of due process, doesn't it?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Well, all I could say that this Court has recognized many such trials as being due process but --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: But if they were to try him for violating that statute which makes it a crime to go abroad if he -- so in this -- it wouldn't be due process to try it without a jury of indictment would it?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: That would be something else, yes sir. But this is a procedure that's recognized in many areas of the determination of rights of citizens as being due process.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: If he -- if on that case, that they charge him with that, he would – he could be fined for $500, would he be entitled to a jury?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Well, it would depend upon the statute. But then --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Well, the statute in the section makes it a crime.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Ordinarily, he would be. Yes, sir.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: If he didn't waive it.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, does that mean to say that constitutionally will offend -- if Congress outlaws conduct to be defined by not loading so many months and not loading so many dollars, that that require a jury trial.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: No, it does not.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: A whole realm of petty offense and he's out of the jury trial.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: What is the line between those, as I recall it, there was once the District of Columbia that (inaudible) has been indicated. It was said the trial that many people might think we're petty but entitle them to use them in fact. I forgot the name it was written by as far as I recall it was Mr. Justice Harlan and many years ago. What are they?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Well, there isn't any question of what is that the line is between petty offenses but the precise line depends on the particular cases I would think. I want to also correct one statement I made about the breadth of action in other countries.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Do you want to consider this a petty offense if it could take away his citizenship as a punishment, wouldn't you?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: No, I think Congress dealt with it as providing in a declaratory judgment that would give him the complete review on the facts. Of course, in this particular case, in the Perez case, there is no issue about the facts. They're all admitted. There isn't any on what the particular act was performed. They admitted it.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Some question about why, isn't it?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: That is --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: If -- if your adversary's position is correct that the law -- the Constitution forbids (Inaudible) or it does not permit the Constitution to take away citizenship which the man has not voluntarily abandoned, assuming at the Perez, would you say that -- then Congress could classify along with those who do not voluntarily (Inaudible) those who do by defining as a ground to forfeit citizenship, voting in a foreign country.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Well, I would say no if you say that you have to find subjective intent. But if you have to find merely that he did evade the draft for this or went to a foreign country and stayed there for the purpose of evading the draft and he admits that fact as he does here then I don't think there's any issue of that. You see, he --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: What I --

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: -- admits that not only that he went to the foreign country and stayed there but that he did it for this very purpose.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: The question I was asking you was following up Mr. Justice Frankfurter's question (Inaudible)

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: -- is the question I'm asking that is there not also an additional power or how do the reference to this legislative power in this respect that if they are right, your adversaries are right, saying there must be a voluntary or using the word to be deliberate, intentional, real honest to goodness abandonment by the person, before Congress can take away citizenship except maybe for a crime. If that be the case, would you still say that Congress could classify the innocent and the guilty in that line or if you want to call it that are the knowing and the unknowing, of course, they lumped and only in one group and say the conduct which might be either could be used to forfeit citizenship, would you say that?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: No. I -- I don't think they could be lumped together if you say that there has to be subjective intent.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Then there wouldn't be any question of reasonableness under those circumstances, would it?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: No. Of course, that concedes their argument --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: I -- I understand --

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: -- but we don't.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: I understood you assuming something if you rigorously deny.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: General, at the outset of your -- of your argument, you impressed on us that we ought to view this case in the light of the facts, namely, that this man was down there in Mexico from the time he was nine or ten years old, that he -- he married a woman, had seven children and -- and a lot of other things happened down in -- in Mexico and you told us that we should take those things into consideration. Now, how can you say then that there wasn't a factual situation for the Court to take into consideration and then -- and that all they had to do was just -- just to determine whether he actually voted down there and whether he actually stayed away.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Well, what I meant was that there was no issue between us that the facts were any different. We -- both sides agree that the facts in this case are that he did live down there, that he did vote in this election and it was a political election --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: -- that he stayed out of the country -- went out of the country and stayed out of the country to evade the draft --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: -- and he conceded all of those. So, there's no issue as to those facts.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: But if you are before a jury, don't you think that -- that the facts as they're presented by each side as to this man living down there and his family and everything and why he came up to -- to America and so forth. And those two occasions he came, don't you think those all might be presented in a different way that might call for a review?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: They shouldn't be. They ought to be the same facts before a jury --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: -- jury --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: -- before a court.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: It's open to -- he never received --

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: I know --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- a proceeding. You know they do --

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: -- and might.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- and besides do present their facts in a little different way.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: May I take a minute of your time or of our time. In this case, the issue arose because he was sought to be deported as an alien, is that right?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: He came in -- he tried to come into the country and they sought --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Sought (Inaudible)

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- not to let him in --

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- because he was an alien.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Now, the -- he then put a -- he put an issue to the fact that he was a citizen.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And the Court decided against him.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: What I would like to know is whether you think the procedure that was just followed here, satisfy and -- and exhaust 503 or could he have brought an independent proceeding or could he now (Inaudible) putting that to one side for the moment, could he now bring an independent proceeding to have that issue determined on the basis of my question which was tied to the deportation case of citizenship which requires merely a judicial proceeding and not a jury trial.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Well, I would think there would be a right available to him under the Declaratory Judgment Act. There would be a justiciable controversy that --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Despite these things or -- or this may -- you -- he may have been said to have had the kind of a judicial proceeding that this Court in (Inaudible) said an issue of citizenship, where is this? I don't know which but I haven't realize, I should have that -- I hadn't realized your Section 503 which whatever else -- whatever have consideration of due process may raised, I think comes within the scope and satisfy the scope that analogy of the -- of the deportation case (Inaudible)

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Well, it seems to me the start of the Section is adequate and fully satisfies the requirements.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Of that -- of what was laid down doesn't touch the jury thing because no jury requirement was made. I hope you can get the impression I'm not -- taking away citizenship is a petty matter.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: And I'm sure.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I meant that if Congress made an offense punishable by not more than a short period, and what is short, nobody in court has direct sense or how much money is petty, but then it isn't within the restriction of the requirements of the Bill of Rights.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: That's right, sir.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And what the limits are, nobody knows, the amount in the quantity punishment, the stigma, in other -- other words the problem is a very (Inaudible)

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Horsky.

turns.name: CHARLES A. HORSKY
turns.text: Just a -- pure words, Your Honor. First, let me speak to this question that you were just discussing as to the facts. I think the court below operated on the assumption which I am sure is the correct assumption under the statute that it's only -- the only findings it had to make were the findings, did this man vote in a political election, did he stay out of the country to avoid the draft? There is nothing in the record and there is nothing that you could find out from even the hearings before the Immigration and Naturalization service into which I looked which would tell you why he did these things. The Solicitor General would have you approach this case as if these two Sections were to be treated as evidentiary in terms of his intention that you take these into account in determining whether this man ought to be held to have abandoned his citizenship. They do not operate in that fashion. They operate automatically. The vote is the fact and that's the end of it. I don't know why he voted in the election. I could think of a lot of reasons why he might have voted, which would have nothing to do with dividing his allegiance or showing any lack of allegiance for United States.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Does it appear what kind of an election it was?

turns.name: CHARLES A. HORSKY
turns.text: I think he voted for President Aleman in the elections of 1946.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: It wasn't here, though. It wasn't here.

turns.name: CHARLES A. HORSKY
turns.text: It wasn't here. Now, let me come to beer, Your Honor. [Laughs] I hope I didn't mislead you but I --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But you didn't at all.

turns.name: CHARLES A. HORSKY
turns.text: That Attorney General did rule that voting on that issue in that election lost -- caused Ms. (Inaudible) her citizenship.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I -- you didn't --

turns.name: CHARLES A. HORSKY
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- at all. You couldn't.

turns.name: CHARLES A. HORSKY
turns.text: [laughs] Well, I thought you had the impression that I have misstated the case in some fashion.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Oh, I -- I'm sorry.

turns.name: CHARLES A. HORSKY
turns.text: I didn't --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Even I (Inaudible)

turns.name: CHARLES A. HORSKY
turns.text: I hoped to shock you. I didn't want to shock you.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well.

turns.name: CHARLES A. HORSKY
turns.text: It Shock me but I didn't want to shock you improperly [laughs].

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But it couldn't. It could be.

turns.name: CHARLES A. HORSKY
turns.text: Now, let me --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Inaudible) except through my hearing of not understanding what you said.

turns.name: CHARLES A. HORSKY
turns.text: Well, I perhaps didn't distinguish between opinions of the Attorney General and --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, I -- I (Voice Overlap) -- with you.

turns.name: CHARLES A. HORSKY
turns.text: -- the ruling of the Attorney General.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But he did say this is a way of getting a ruling from the Court.

turns.name: CHARLES A. HORSKY
turns.text: He said, if she wants to go to court, she can.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, he said it's undecided and I don't know. It's not --

turns.name: CHARLES A. HORSKY
turns.text: But I'm going to say that she loses her citizenship. If she wants to go to court, she can. If she wants to come and be renatularized, she can.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: To me --

turns.name: CHARLES A. HORSKY
turns.text: But she's out --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- it's illustration of the excessive burdens on an Attorney General attack on a case like this.

turns.name: CHARLES A. HORSKY
turns.text: I should think he should not be allowed to, either. I've heard the Court of that. One other item which is related to the matter which we were discussing as to this man's dual citizenship, there is no finding by the Court that he was a dual national, either by the Court or by the bureau -- or by Immigration and Naturalization Service. One could construct a theory upon which he would be, I concede that. I could construct the theory upon which he would not. It, however, it seems to me that it must be irrelevant as Your Honor pointed out in the questions. Whether a man's parents were American-born or foreign-born as to what his rights are under the Constitution, I think that issue must be an irrelevant issue. I would concede that the proposition which I state in terms of what a man may do or may not do in order to abandon citizenship might have to be phrased somewhat differently in the case of a man who involuntarily was a dual national, who, because of the nature of his parents' birth was claimed by Italy or France or Japan or what have you, it might make a difference. But it would only be a matter of phraseology. His substantive rights under the Constitution must be the same. Now, one more word and I'll be done. The Solicitor General has referred to the necessity for the United States to have the attributes of sovereignty. Of course, it must have the attributes of sovereignty and it does. But certainly, it is not and has never been the law that in exercising those attributes of sovereignty, the United States may act in derogation of constitutional rights. I suppose it is true almost everywhere in the world that a nation can legislate about church matters. Most nations do not separate church and state. That that certainly does not mean that in the United States, because you can point to all the other places where the Congress or the Legislature or the Executive can deal with the church, that Congress here can avoid the First Amendment. We have here --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But we have controversies as to when it does and when it doesn't.

turns.name: CHARLES A. HORSKY
turns.text: Well, we do. But we certainly admit that it is still effective, notwithstanding, the nature of sovereignty and the existence of the similar power in other countries. What we have here but I cannot say it too much and too sincerely, is a constitutionally protected right. A very important constitutionally protected right, native-born citizenship. It seems to me impossible to believe that that right can be at the discretion of Congress to take away for as light or as grave a reason as it sees fit. I submit that the decision below should be reversed.
